issue.title: Updates to reflect changes to RFC7523 (jwt_privatekey attack)
issue.body: Remove reference to token endpoint in JWT authorization grant section to protect against malicious authorization servers (see https://github.com/selfissued/draft-jones-oauth-rfc7523bis )

issue.title: Updates to reflect changes to RFC7523 (jwt_privatekey attack)
issue.body: Remove reference to token endpoint in JWT authorization grant section to protect against malicious authorization servers (see https://github.com/selfissued/draft-jones-oauth-rfc7523bis )

comment.body:
> Having recently done a full read of the draft and having some familiarity with the content and direction of the draft-ietf-oauth-rfc7523bis work, I am not convinced there are changes needed here.

I believe no changes needed here.

issue.title: Question: DPoP proof handling across trust domain boundaries
issue.body:
## Question

draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining mentions DPoP in Appendix B.3 but I'm 
trying to understand the intended behavior for a specific scenario.

## Scenario

Multi-hop authentication where:
1. User authenticates to Service A with DPoP-bound token
2. Service A needs to exchange that token with Domain B's AS
3. New token should be DPoP-bound to Service A's key

## The htu binding question

When the user authenticated to Service A, their DPoP proof had:
```
{ "htu": "https://service-a.example.com/auth", "htm": "POST" }
```

If Service A wants to prove to Domain B's AS that the user's DPoP was
verified, what's the expected mechanism?

Options I'm considering:

**(A)** Service A forwards user's original proof
- Problem: htu doesn't match Domain B's token endpoint

**(B)** Service A attests "I verified the user's DPoP"  
- Problem: Domain B must trust Service A's assertion

**(C)** User pre-generates proof for Domain B's endpoint
- Problem: User must know exchange flow in advance

## Questions

1. Is there a recommended approach for this scenario, or is it intentionally
   left to implementers?
2. If it's left to implementers, are there any pitfalls to avoid?

Happy to help if this is something the WG wants to address.

---

**References**:
- [Mailing list: DPoP for token exchange](https://www.mail-archive.com/oauth@ietf.org/msg24953.html)
- [Appendix B.3 - Delegated Key Binding](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining#appendix-B.3)

issue.title: Question: DPoP proof handling across trust domain boundaries
issue.body:
## Question

draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining mentions DPoP in Appendix B.3 but I'm 
trying to understand the intended behavior for a specific scenario.

## Scenario

Multi-hop authentication where:
1. User authenticates to Service A with DPoP-bound token
2. Service A needs to exchange that token with Domain B's AS
3. New token should be DPoP-bound to Service A's key

## The htu binding question

When the user authenticated to Service A, their DPoP proof had:
```
{ "htu": "https://service-a.example.com/auth", "htm": "POST" }
```

If Service A wants to prove to Domain B's AS that the user's DPoP was
verified, what's the expected mechanism?

Options I'm considering:

**(A)** Service A forwards user's original proof
- Problem: htu doesn't match Domain B's token endpoint

**(B)** Service A attests "I verified the user's DPoP"  
- Problem: Domain B must trust Service A's assertion

**(C)** User pre-generates proof for Domain B's endpoint
- Problem: User must know exchange flow in advance

## Questions

1. Is there a recommended approach for this scenario, or is it intentionally
   left to implementers?
2. If it's left to implementers, are there any pitfalls to avoid?

Happy to help if this is something the WG wants to address.

---

**References**:
- [Mailing list: DPoP for token exchange](https://www.mail-archive.com/oauth@ietf.org/msg24953.html)
- [Appendix B.3 - Delegated Key Binding](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining#appendix-B.3)

comment.body:
This does seem intentionally out of scope for these specs, if I understand it, which I'm not sure I really do. 

But also note that this draft is in the later stages of the publication process and not really a good candidate for much change at this point.

@@ -303,46 +304,46 @@ identity_chaining_requested_token_types_supported
 
 ## OAuth Authorization Server Metadata Registry
 
-This specification defines the following parameter in the "OAuth Authorization Server Metadata" registry established in {{RFC8414}}.
+This specification requests registration the following parameter in the "OAuth Authorization Server Metadata" registry established in {{RFC8414}}.

-It is RECOMMENDED that any profile or deployment-specific implementation adopt explicit typing as defined in JSON Web Token Best Current Practices {{RFC8725}} and define a new media type {{RFC2046}} in the "Media Types" registry {{IANA.media-types}} in the manner described in {{RFC6838}}.

comment.body:
Can you elaborate on the purpose? 

Having read RFC 9700 Section 5.2 (https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9700.html#name-client-authentication) it does not preclude the use of shared secrets - just recommends the use of client authentication if the deployment allows for it ,which is plenty of wiggle room for when you can't. 

We can explicitly refernece Section 2.5 if we are worried that RFC 9700 might be misread on the topic:

>2.5.  Client Authentication

   Authorization servers SHOULD enforce client authentication if it is
   feasible, in the particular deployment, to establish a process for
   issuance/registration of credentials for clients and ensuring the
   confidentiality of those credentials.

   It is RECOMMENDED to use asymmetric cryptography for client
   authentication, such as mutual TLS for OAuth 2.0 [RFC8705] or signed
   JWTs ("Private Key JWT") in accordance with [RFC7521] and [RFC7523].
   The latter is defined in [OpenID.Core] as the client authentication
   method private_key_jwt).  When asymmetric cryptography for client
   authentication is used, authorization servers do not need to store
   sensitive symmetric keys, making these methods more robust against
   leakage of keys.

comment.body:
I was curious about how OAuth 2.1 uses RFC 9700, and interestingly enough, it never says you SHOULD apply RFC 9700. At most it refers the reader to it for a specific topic. Perhaps we just need to point to that section. to leave as little as possible to the readers imagination?
 
```suggestion
Authorization Servers should follow Section 2.5 of the Best Current Practice for OAuth 2.0 Security {{RFC9700}} for client authentication.
```

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -538,12 +539,16 @@ However, the trust relationship between the authorization server in trust domain
 * The client in trust domain A that presents the access token must use the key matching the "cnf" claim to generate a DPoP proof or setup a MTLS session when presenting the access token to a resource server in in trust domain B.
 
 # Acknowledgements {#Acknowledgements}
-The editors would like to thank Patrick Harding, Joe Jubinski, Watson Ladd, Justin Richer, Adam Rusbridge, Dean H. Saxe, and others (please let us know, if you've been mistakenly omitted) for their valuable input, feedback and general support of this work.
+The editors would like to thank Deb Cooley, Patrick Harding, Joe Jubinski, Watson Ladd, Justin Richer, Adam Rusbridge, Dean H. Saxe, and others (please let us know, if you've been mistakenly omitted) for their valuable input, feedback and general support of this work.
comment.body:
I did move Mr. @aaronpk to the [Contributors](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining-08#name-contributors) section sometime during that journey. At this point to be specific: [Move Mr. Parecki from acknowledgements to contributors in acknowledgement of his contributions](https://github.com/oauth-wg/oauth-identity-chaining/pull/163). The idea there was that Contributors is a bit higher level of acknowledgment than the Acknowledgments and I thought Aaron worthy of such acknowledgment in the context of this work.

I did not consider the appropriateness of the location of any other names when I did that. I know the larger question naturally follows but I purposefully ignored it in favor of just wanting to take care of Aaron with an easy change. But maybe that's something we need to look at now. 

At the time, I did consider adding Mr. @aaronpk to the authors list but was dissuaded by the soft limit of 5 authors per doc. I would be very open to revisiting that decision.

comment.body: yeah but also that one isn't totally "naked" but even more so I have a personal preference for less arbitrary use of the big caps words and this SHOULD as a little should seemed like a good thing and I thought Deb's comments gave me some cover to do things like this
